question: [File: 'src/periphery/TakeAmountsLib.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: buyerAssetsToUnits, sellerAssetsToUnits] Can an unprivileged attacker cause MidnightBundles to revert by exploiting the fact that the library reads settlementFee(id, ttm) where ttm is computed from offer.market.maturity, but the actual market stored on-chain (via toMarket(id)) may have a different maturity if the id was computed from a different chain (INITIAL_CHAIN_ID mismatch), causing the library to use a wrong TTM and wrong fee? Preconditions: offer.market.maturity is set correctly for the current chain; id is computed from a different chain's parameters; settlementFee(id, ttm) uses the wrong market's fee breakpoints. Call sequence: (1) attacker crafts offer with id from different chain; (2) buyerAssetsToUnits calls settlementFee(wrong_id, ttm); (3) returns wrong units; (4) take() uses correct id and different fee; (5) actual buyerAssets != targetBuyerAssets; (6) bundle reverts. Invariant tested: library must use the same id as Midnight's take() for fee computation. Scoped impact: DoS of take via periphery for cross-chain id confusion. Proof idea: unit test with mismatched chain id, assert bundle reverts.

[File: 'src/periphery/TakeAmountsLib.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: buyerAssetsToUnits] Can an unprivileged attacker cause MidnightBundles.buyWithAssetsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral to revert by exploiting the fact that the bundler's min() function (taking three arguments) returns 0 when any of the three arguments is 0, and if ConsumableUnitsLib.consumableUnits returns 0 (offer fully consumed), the take is called with 0 units, which reverts in Midnight (or is a no-op), and the loop skips the offer, eventually causing OutOfOffers? Preconditions: all offers in takes[] are fully consumed (consumed >= maxUnits or consumed >= maxAssets); victim calls bundle expecting to fill targetUnits. Call sequence: (1) attacker front-runs and exhausts all offers; (2) consumableUnits returns 0 for each offer; (3) min(remaining, takes[i].units, 0) = 0; (4) take(0) reverts; (5) all offers skipped; (6) OutOfOffers. Invariant tested: bundle must not be DoS-able by exhausting all offers. Scoped impact: low-cost DoS of take via periphery by front-running to exhaust offers. Proof idea: stateful fuzz test with attacker exhausting offers before victim's bundle.

[File: 'src/periphery/TakeAmountsLib.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: sellerAssetsToUnits] Can an unprivileged attacker cause MidnightBundles.supplyCollateralAndSellWithAssetsTarget to revert by exploiting the fact that the bundler calls supplyCollateral before the take loop, and if the take loop fails (OutOfOffers), the collateral is already deposited but the loan tokens are not received, effectively locking the taker's collateral in the protocol until they manually withdraw? Preconditions: attacker front-runs to exhaust all offers in takes[]; victim calls supplyCollateralAndSellWithAssetsTarget; collateral is deposited but no loan tokens received. Call sequence: (1) attacker exhausts all offers; (2) victim calls bundle; (3) supplyCollateral deposits collateral; (4) take loop fails; (5) require(filledSellerAssets == targetFilledSellerAssets) reverts; (6) collateral deposit is reverted (atomically). Invariant tested: bundle must be atomic - collateral deposit must be reverted if take fails. Scoped impact: DoS of sell-via-periphery with collateral temporarily locked (but reverted atomically). Proof idea: unit test confirming atomicity of bundle revert.

[File: 'src/periphery/TakeAmountsLib.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: buyerAssetsToUnits, sellerAssetsToUnits] Can an unprivileged attacker cause MidnightBundles to revert by exploiting the fact that the library uses TickLib.tickToPrice(offer.tick) which reverts with TickOutOfRange if offer.tick > MAX_TICK=5820, and since this revert is not caught by try/catch in the bundler, the entire bundle reverts? Preconditions: attacker crafts offer with tick > MAX_TICK; victim includes this offer in takes[]. Call sequence: (1) buyerAssetsToUnits calls tickToPrice(offer.tick > 5820); (2) tickToPrice reverts with TickOutOfRange; (3) bundle reverts (not caught). Invariant tested: bundler must validate tick range before calling TakeAmountsLib. Scoped impact: DoS of take via periphery by including out-of-range tick offers. Proof idea: unit test with tick > MAX_TICK, assert bundle reverts.
